title: Moses Itauma is 21. Fabio Wardley is the test that tells us how real he is.
summary: Fourteen fights, fourteen wins, a five-fight knockout streak, and Moses Itauma is only 21. Fabio Wardley is the first opponent built to find out what is underneath the hype.
body:
Moses Itauma is 21 years old, and that single fact reframes everything else on his record. Fourteen fights, fourteen wins, twelve by knockout, and a last five that reads as five straight stoppages. Plenty of heavyweights spend their early twenties learning the trade. Itauma has spent his ending nights early. Fabio Wardley is the first opponent built to find out what sits beneath that.

The prospect

Itauma's appeal is in the package. An 86 per cent knockout rate, a southpaw stance and a 79-inch reach make him an awkward, heavy-handed problem at 6ft 4in. The caveat is just as plain. Fourteen professional fights is a short education, and none of them has forced him into the deep, ugly rounds that define the division.

The proven puncher

Wardley, 31, is the more battle-tested article. His 20-1-1 record carries 19 knockouts, a 95 per cent finishing rate of his own. His recent five tells a rawer story than Itauma's tidy run, taking in a knockout loss, a sequence of stoppage wins and a draw. He has lived in the chaotic, momentum-swinging rounds that the younger man's record has never required.

What we actually learn

This is an audition with real stakes. Itauma brings the southpaw stance, a marginal reach edge and a decade of youth, since Wardley is ten years older. Wardley brings scars and the hard-won knowledge of what to do when a fight stops going to plan. Both can clearly punch. What nobody has seen yet is how Itauma reacts the first time a night refuses to go his way, and Wardley is built to ask exactly that.
